title: Metaphysics XII: The Unmoved Mover
description: Aristotle's theology: God as pure thought thinking itself, the first cause that moves all things by being desired.
introduction: In Book XII (Lambda) of the Metaphysics, Aristotle arrives at the summit of his philosophy: the Unmoved Mover, a being of pure actuality with no potentiality, whose only activity is thinking itself. This God does not create the world but moves it by being the object of cosmic desire — all things strive toward the perfection that the Unmoved Mover already is. The concept of 'thinking thinking itself' (noēsis noēseōs noēsis) became central to Islamic, Jewish, and Christian theology for two millennia.

translation.original_text: ἔστι τι ὃ οὐ κινούμενον κινεῖ, ἀΐδιον καὶ οὐσία καὶ ἐνέργεια οὖσα. κινεῖ δὲ ὡς ἐρώμενον.
translation.text: There is something which moves without being moved, being eternal, and both substance and actuality. It moves as an object of desire.

translation.original_text: ἡ γὰρ νοῦ ἐνέργεια ζωή, ἐκεῖνος δὲ ἡ ἐνέργεια· ἐνέργεια δὲ ἡ καθ᾿ αὑτὴν ἐκείνου ζωὴ ἀρίστη καὶ ἀΐδιος.
translation.text: The activity of Mind is life, and God is that activity. God's self-dependent activity is life most good and eternal.

translation.original_text: αὑτὸν ἄρα νοεῖ, εἴπερ ἐστὶ τὸ κράτιστον, καὶ ἔστιν ἡ νόησις νοήσεως νόησις.
translation.text: It thinks itself, if indeed it is the most excellent thing, and its thinking is a thinking of thinking.
